Appendix 2
Formulary
Anna Meredith
The majority of drugs are still not authorized for use in  presented in Figure A2.1 reflect those given by the chapter 
guinea pigs and practitioners should always use their dis- authors and some may differ from other formularies. Pub-
cretion, acquire informed owner consent and follow rele- lished pharmacological and efficacy evidence is frequently 
vant prescribing legislation (the prescribing cascade in the  from controlled laboratory animal studies rather than clini-
UK). Although the body of knowledge is longstanding and  cal practice-based trials. Antibiotics that have a primarily 
continues to grow, practitioners should recognize that many  Gram-positive spectrum of activity should be avoided in 
doses are extrapolated from use in other species or from  guinea pigs as they may cause fatal dysbiosis.
anecdotal use based on clinical outcome. Doses and uses 
Drug Dose and route Comments
Anaesthetics, analgesics and NSAIDs (see also Chapter 7 for protocols)
Alfaxalone ■  1–3 mg/kg i.m. or i.v./i.o. slowly to effect As part of combined anaesthesia (see Chapter 7), 
dose assumes premedication
Transient apnoea may occur if given rapidly by 
i.v. injection
Note: published doses are often much higher, up 
to 40 mg/kg i.m., i.p. as a sole agent. However, 
doses of 6–8 mg/kg have been associated with 
adverse effects including tachycardia and 
hypotension
Not considered an analgesic agent and not 
adequate alone for painful procedures
Bupivacaine ■  2 mg/kg perineural/infiltration For specific nerve blocks (see Chapter 7)
May require dilution with saline for local infusion
Can be combined with lidocaine
Buprenorphine ■  0.05–0.2 mg/kg i.m., s.c. q4–8h as required;  For visceral pain
oral transmucosal route q4h Can be used to treat cardiorespiratory disorders
Can be combined with other drugs for multi-
modal analgesia and anaesthesia                       
(see Chapter 7)
The higher end of the dose range may be 
required for full analgesic effect but should be 
used with caution as individual responses are 
variable and sedation may occur
Butorphanol ■  0.2–2.0 mg/kg i.m., s.c. q12h  Some sedative effect; useful to complete 
■  0.1–0.2 mg/kg/h i.v. CRI with a loading dose  examination and diagnostic tests
of 0.02 mg/kg i.v. Often combined with midazolam
Can be used to treat cardiorespiratory disorders
Carprofen ■  1–2 mg/kg p.o. q12h
■  4 mg/kg s.c. q12–24h
Dexmedetomidine ■  0.005–0.25 mg/kg i.v., i.o. (lower dose) to  Generally given as part of a combination when 
effect or i.m. used for sedation; can be combined with an 
opioid or NSAID (see Chapter 7)
ACTH = adrenocorticotropic hormone; CRI = continuous rate infusion; i.c. = intracoelomic; i.m. = intramuscular; i.o. = intraosseous; 
A2.1
i.p. = intraperitoneal; i.v. = intravenous; NSAID = non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug; p.o. = by mouth, orally; s.c. = subcutaneous. 
(continues)

Appendix 2 • Formulary
Drug Dose and route Comments
Diazepam For seizures: Can be reversed with flumazenil (see below)
■  0.5–3 mg/kg i.v., rectally once (acute control 
of seizures or head tilt)
■  0.5–1 mg/kg i.v., i.o., repeat every 5 minutes if 
seizures recur, up to three times
■  1–2 mg/kg/h i.v. CRI (if seizures are still not 
controlled or respond to treatment but recur)
For sedation:
■  0.5–5 mg/kg i.m. as required
Diclofenac ■  0.1% solution, 1 drop topically q4h, or q30min  Topical analgesia for ophthalmological 
for 2 hours prior to ophthalmic surgery conditions
Fentanyl ■  2–7 µg (micrograms)/kg/h i.v. CRI Severe visceral pain
Can be used in combination with medetomidine 
and midazolam (see Chapter 7)
Flurbiprofen ■  0.03% solution, 1 drop topically q4h, or  Topical analgesia for ophthalmological 
q30min for 2 hours prior to ophthalmic  conditions
surgery
Gabapentin ■  3–5 mg/kg p.o. q8–24h (seizures) Adjunctive treatment for refractory seizures
■  10–30 mg/kg p.o. q12–24h (neuropathic pain) Mild sedation
Hydrocortisone ■  1% cream, apply topically as required Multiple indications where local anti-inflamma-
tory action is required (e.g. infiltration into 
perineal sac following clearance)
Hydromorphone ■  0.3 mg/kg i.v. q2–3h, i.m. q5–6h
■  0.035–0.05 mg/kg/h i.v. CRI with a loading 
dose of 0.05 mg/kg i.v.
Ketamine ■  See Chapter 7 Given as part of combined anaesthesia
Ketorolac ■  0.5% solution, 1 drop topically q6–24h  NSAID used for topical analgesia for ophthalmo-
depending on severity of inflammation logical conditions
Lidocaine/lignocaine ■  Dilute (2%): apply topically as required For irrigating the rectum and perineal sac
■  Gel (1–2%): apply topically as required For lubrication of the tip of nasogastric tubes for 
■  5 mg/kg perineural/infiltration (local anaes- a more comfortable passage
thesia combined with bupivacaine)
For use on the skin or rectal area prior to tube 
■  1–2 mg/kg i.v. bolus (antiarrhythmic)
placement for enema or transfaunation
■  3 mg/kg/h i.v. CRI (motility enhancer)
For specific nerve blocks (see Chapter 7) (can 
be combined with bupivacaine)
Consider total dose absorbed with topical use 
on multiple areas
Median lethal dose (LD50) of i.v. lidocaine in 
guinea pigs has been reported as 24 mg/kg
Medetomidine ■  0.1–0.2 mg/kg i.m. Generally given as part of a combination when 
used for sedation; can be combined with an 
opioid or NSAID (see Chapter 7)
Meloxicam ■  0.2 mg/kg p.o. q24h (authorized dose) Authorized dose: 0.2 mg/kg q24h on day 1 and 
■  1.5 mg/kg p.o. q12h (see comments) 0.1 mg/kg subsequently, with doses of up to 0.5 
■  1.5 mg/kg i.m., s.c. (see comments) mg/kg on an individual patient basis where 
necessary
A dose of 1.5 mg/kg has been shown to reach 
therapeutic levels with a short half-life (Sadar 
and Mans, 2023)
A small-scale pharmacokinetic study by 
Moeremans et al. (2019) showed that a dose of 
1.5 mg/kg orally reached similar plasma levels to 
those seen in rabbits following a 1 mg/kg 
meloxicam oral dose but with a shorter half-life, 
suggesting more frequent dosing intervals are 
required
Safety of doses >0.6 mg/kg has not been 
evaluated in guinea pigs
The oral route is less effective in animals with 
gastrointestinal disease
Can be combined with an opioid (see Chapter 7)
Metamizole ■  65 mg/kg s.c., i.v., p.o. q8–12h
(continued) ACTH = adrenocorticotropic hormone; CRI = continuous rate infusion; i.c. = intracoelomic; i.m. = intramuscular; i.o. = 
A2.1
intraosseous; i.p. = intraperitoneal; i.v. = intravenous; NSAID = non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug; p.o. = by mouth, orally; s.c. = 
subcutaneous. (continues)

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Drug Dose and route Comments
Methadone ■  1–2 mg/kg s.c. q4h as required For visceral pain
Midazolam ■  0.5–1.0 mg/kg i.m., i.v., s.c. q8–12h For relaxation, sedation and anti-anxiety
■  0.25–2 mg/kg s.c., i.m. for treatment of  Can be combined with an opioid or NSAID (see 
seizures or distress, such as with acute    Chapter 7)
head tilt
Doses of up to 5 mg/kg i.v., i.m. have been 
reported
Morphine ■  1–5 mg/kg s.c. q4h For visceral pain
Nalbuphine ■  1% solution, 1 drop topically q8h Use injectable solution
Topical analgesia for ophthalmological 
conditions if corneal ulceration is present
Oxymorphone ■  0.2–0.5 mg/kg s.c. q4h For visceral pain
Propofol ■  5–7 mg/kg i.v. once Induction agent for general anaesthesia
Tramadol ■  4–10 mg/kg p.o. q12h Wean off slowly if stopping after long-term use
Antibiotics
Amoxicillin – Do not use
Amoxicillin/Clavulanic Acid  – Do not use
Ampicillin – Do not use
Azithromycin ■  15–30 mg/kg p.o. q24h Respiratory infections
Cefalexin – Do not use
Cefotaxime – Do not use
Cefoxitin – Do not use
Ceftazidime – Do not use
Ceftiofur – Do not use
Cephalothin – Do not use
Chloramphenicol ■  30–50 mg/kg i.m., s.c., i.v., p.o. q8–12h For Tyzzer’s disease, other clostridial and 
Gram-negative bacteria
Treatment of otitis externa
Has a bitter taste – chloramphenicol palmitate is 
better tolerated for oral use
Chlortetracycline  ■  50 mg/kg p.o. q12h Bacteriostatic
Ciprofloxacin ■  5–25 mg/kg p.o. q12h Topical antibiotic for ophthalmological 
■  Ophthalmic: 1 drop q6h with a loading dose of  conditions
1 drop q15min for 4 doses Ophthalmic dose extrapolated from use in 
rabbits
Clindamycin – Do not use
Doxycycline ■  2.5 mg/kg p.o. q12h Bacteriostatic
■  70–100 mg/kg s.c., i.m. (long-acting  Treatment of otitis externa and Chlamydia 
preparation) caviae
Enrofloxacin ■  10–20 mg/kg s.c., i.m., i.v., p.o. q12h Important antibiotic in human medicine. Only use 
if no other alternatives
Erythromycin – Do not use
Fusidic acid ■  Ophthalmic: 10 mg/kg, 1 drop q12–24h Steroid-containing preparations should be used 
with caution due to unpredictable dermal 
absorption and risk of ingestion while grooming
Gentamicin ■  6 mg/kg s.c. q24h Use with caution systemically (risk of dysbiosis)
■  Ophthalmic: 0.5% solution, 1 drop q6–8h Topical antibiotic for ophthalmological 
conditions
Component of some topical ear preparations
Ineffective against Chlamydia spp.
Lincomycin – Do not use
Marbofloxacin ■  2–5 mg/kg p.o., s.c., i.m. q24h Treatment of otitis externa, respiratory, urinary, 
skin and soft tissue infections based on 
susceptibility testing
(continued) ACTH = adrenocorticotropic hormone; CRI = continuous rate infusion; i.c. = intracoelomic; i.m. = intramuscular; i.o. = 
A2.1
intraosseous; i.p. = intraperitoneal; i.v. = intravenous; NSAID = non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug; p.o. = by mouth, orally; s.c. = 
subcutaneous. (continues)

Appendix 2 • Formulary
Drug Dose and route Comments
Metronidazole ■  10–20 mg/kg p.o. q12h Treatment of antibiotic-induced dysbiosis
■  40 mg/kg p.o. q24h
Oxytetracycline  ■  Ophthalmic: apply ointment q6–12h
■  50 mg/kg p.o. q12h
■  5 mg/kg i.m. q12h
Silver sulfadiazine cream ■  Topically q8–12h Antibacterial and antifungal, used for second- 
and third-degree burns and skin wounds
Used in the perineal sac after clearing
Do not use in neonates or pregnant animals
Tetracycline ■  10 mg/kg p.o. q8–12h (treatment of Tyzzer’s  Chlamydia caviae retinal disease
disease)
■  Ophthalmic: 3% ointment, apply q8–12h
Tobramycin ■  Ointment: q8–12h Topical antibiotic for ophthalmological 
■  Drops: q4–6h conditions
Ineffective against Chlamydia spp.
Trimethoprim/sulphonamide ■  30–50 mg/kg p.o. q12h Bacteriostatic
Anticoccidial
Primarily for Gram-negative bacteria
Tylosin – Do not use
Vancomycin ■  20 mg/kg p.o. q24h For Clostridioides difficile and other Clostridia
No formulation of vancomycin is approved for 
use in any veterinary species. Use is discour-
aged due to its role as a last-resort antibiotic for 
multi-resistant Gram-positive bacteria in humans
Antifungals
Enilconazole  ■  0.2% topically q7d Dermatophytosis
Fluconazole  ■  15–20 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 14 days Dermatophytosis
Griseofulvin ■  1.5% in dimethylsulfoxide topically q24h for  Dermatophytosis
5–7 days
■  25 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 3–5 weeks
■  In feed: 0.75 mg/kg for 3–5 weeks
■  250 mg/kg paediatric suspension p.o. q10d 
for 3 treatments
Itraconazole ■  5–20 mg/kg p.o. q24h Dermatophytosis
Gastrointestinal and cutaneous candidiasis
Ketoconazole ■  10–40 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 14 days
Miconazole ■  2% cream topically q24h for 2–4 weeks
■  0.2% shampoo topically once or twice weekly
Mupirocin ■  2% cream topically q24h for 2–4 weeks
Nystatin ■  60,000–90,000 IU/kg p.o. q12h for 7–10 days Oral yeast treatment
Ineffective for cheilitis
Works topically – not systemically absorbed
Terbinafine ■  20–40 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 4–6 weeks  Dermatophytosis and yeast infections associ-
(dermatophytosis) or 14 days (other fungus) ated with cheilitis
Antiprotozoals
Sulfadimethoxine ■  25–50 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 10 days Coccidiosis
Toltrazuril ■  10–20 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 2–3 days Coccidiosis
Parasiticides
Amitraz ■  250 ppm topically q7d until 4 weeks after  Demodex caviae
negative skin scrape Largely superseded by ivermectin, selamectin 
and isoxazolines
(continued) ACTH = adrenocorticotropic hormone; CRI = continuous rate infusion; i.c. = intracoelomic; i.m. = intramuscular; i.o. = 
A2.1
intraosseous; i.p. = intraperitoneal; i.v. = intravenous; NSAID = non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug; p.o. = by mouth, orally; s.c. = 
subcutaneous. (continues)

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Drug Dose and route Comments
Doramectin ■  0.5 mg/kg s.c. q7d for 3 treatments
Fenbendazole ■  20 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 5 days
Fipronil ■  3 mg/kg topical spray
Imidacloprid ■  0.05 ml topical solution containing 10% w/v, 
combined with moxidectin
Ivermectin ■  450 µg (micrograms) authorized topical  Topical spray as a preventative measure for 
spot-on colony situations showing no clinical signs of 
■  0.2–0.5 mg/kg p.o., s.c. q7–10d for 3–4  ectoparasites
treatments
■  2 mg/ml topical spray 
Moxidectin ■  0.5 mg/kg s.c. q7d for 3 treatments
■  0.05 ml topical solution containing 1% w/v, 
combined with imidacloprid
Permethrin ■  Spray or wash Often combined with other insecticides (e.g. 
■  0.25% powder in cage imidacloprid)
Praziquantel ■  10 mg/kg s.c., p.o., repeat once after 10 days Cestode and trematode infection
Pyrethrin ■  Spray, wash, powder topically q7d for 3 
treatments
Selamectin ■  15 mg/kg topically once or q14d for 3 
treatments
Miscellaneous
Activated charcoal ■  1–3 mg/g (1–3 g/kg) p.o. Gastrointestinal decontamination (see Appendix 3)
Ambroxol ■  30 mg/kg p.o. q8–12h Mucolytic
Atenolol ■  3–6 mg/kg p.o. q24h Beta blocker
Atropine ■  0.1–0.2 mg/kg s.c., i.m.
■  Ophthalmic: 1% solution topically q12–24h
Benazepril ■  0.05–0.1 mg/kg p.o. q24h Improving clinical signs of proteinuria
Vasodilator
Editor’s note [DM]: 0.25 mg/kg q24h can be 
effective (extrapolated from doses used in 
dogs). Use of doses up to 0.5 mg/kg q24h has 
been reported
Bromhexine ■  5–10 mg/kg s.c., i.m., i.v. q8–12h Mucolytic
Calcium gluconate ■  50 mg/kg i.v. slowly, once Pregnancy toxaemia
■  100 mg/kg i.m. once Monitor the heart during i.v. injection
Carbimazole ■  1–2 mg/kg p.o. q12–24h Thyroid neoplasia
Carvedilol ■  Start at 0.2 mg/kg p.o. q24h then increase to  Beta blocker
0.4 mg/kg p.o. q24h after 2 weeks
Cholestyramine ■  100 mg/ml drinking water (can add to  Binder for clostridial toxins
nutritional formulation)
Cimetidine ■  5–10 mg/kg i.m., i.v., s.c., p.o. q6–12h When gastric ulceration may be present
Cisapride ■  0.1–0.5 mg/kg p.o. q8–12h Motility enhancer and treatment of ileus
Has more hindgut action than metoclopramide
Has to be compounded, or only available as 
tablets
Dexamethasone ■  0.5–2 mg/kg s.c., i.m., i.v. Steroid – use with care
Dextrose (50%) ■  1–2 ml/kg i.v., i.o., p.o. (acute treatment for  Dilute for i.v. and i.o. administration
seizures or head tilt)
Digoxin ■  0.125 mg/kg i.v. Inotrope
Diltiazem ■  1.5–7.5 mg/kg p.o. q12h Calcium channel blocker
(continued) ACTH = adrenocorticotropic hormone; CRI = continuous rate infusion; i.c. = intracoelomic; i.m. = intramuscular; i.o. = 
A2.1
intraosseous; i.p. = intraperitoneal; i.v. = intravenous; NSAID = non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug; p.o. = by mouth, orally; s.c. = 
subcutaneous. (continues)

Appendix 2 • Formulary
Drug Dose and route Comments
Diphenhydramine ■  2–4 mg/kg s.c., p.o. q8–12h Antihistamine
Dopamine ■  0.08 mg/kg i.v.  Inotrope
■  5–10 μg (micrograms)/kg/min i.v. CRI
Enalapril ■  0.25–0.5 mg/kg p.o. q24–48h Vasodilator
Famotidine ■  0.4–0.5 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 7–10 days Gastric protectant. Works by decreasing acid 
secretion stimulation
Flumazenil ■  0.1 mg/kg i.m., s.c. Benzodiazepine antagonist
Furosemide ■  1–4 mg/kg s.c., i.m., i.v., p.o. q8–12h Diuretic
Hydroxyzine ■  2 mg/kg p.o. q8–12h Antihistamine
Insulin glargine ■  0.5–0.75 IU s.c. q1–2d Diabetes mellitus
Iodine (131I; radioactive iodine) ■  1–1.3 mCi (millicurie) s.c. once Thyroid neoplasia
Levetiracetam ■  20 mg/kg p.o. q8h Long-term seizure management
Levothyroxine ■  10–20 μg (micrograms)/kg q24h Hypothyroidism. Periodic monitoring of the total 
thyroxine concentration is required
Maropitant ■  2–4 mg/kg p.o. q24h Presumed nausea
Appears to be effective as an additional 
anti-inflammatory agent in cases of idiopathic 
cystitis that are severe or do not respond to 
initial treatment
Meclizine ■  12.5–25 mg/kg p.o. Acute treatment for seizures or head tilt
Methylprednisolone ■  10–20 mg/kg i.m., i.v. q12–24h
Metoclopramide ■  0.08 mg/kg/h i.v. CRI Motility enhancer
■  0.2–1 mg/kg i.m., s.c., p.o. q8–12h
N-acetylcysteine ■  100–200 mg/kg i.v. Mucolytic
■  100–200 mg/kg nebulization (dilute in 10 ml  Reduces tear viscosity, ocular drops are often 
saline) combined with hypromellose (artificial tears)
■  5% drops for ophthalmological conditions: 
1–2 drops topically q2–6h
Oxytocin ■  1–3 IU/kg s.c., i.m., i.v. Unobstructed dystocia. Caesarean indicated if 
no young delivered after 15 minutes
Pentosan polysulphate ■  3 mg/kg s.c. q7d for 4 weeks Osteoarthritis
Idiopathic cystitis not responsive to NSAIDs
Phenobarbital ■  2–10 mg/kg i.v., i.o., i.m. (seizure control when  Long-term seizure management. Measure levels 
refractory to benzodiazepines) 2–3 weeks after initiation of therapy and then 
■  2–8 mg/kg/h i.v. CRI (if seizures still not  every 6 months. No standard values have been 
controlled) published for guinea pigs
■  2–5 mg/kg p.o. q8–12h (long-term seizure 
management)
Pimobendan ■  0.1–0.3 mg/kg p.o. q24h Inotrope
Potassium bromide ■  100 mg/kg p.o. q24h for 3–5 days as a  Long-term seizure management. Measure levels 
loading dose, then 30 mg/kg p.o. q24h 2–3 months after initiation of therapy, then at 3 
months, then every 6 months. No standard 
values have been published for guinea pigs
Prednisolone ■  1.25–2.5 mg/kg p.o. q24h Ophthalmic use: Suspected allergic conjunctivi-
■  Drops or ointment: 1%, 1 drop topically  tis without corneal ulceration
q4–24h Short-term use only, taper
Palliative treatment for lymphoma
Propranolol ■  0.5–2 mg/kg p.o., s.c. q12–24h Beta blocker
Ranitidine ■  2 mg/kg i.v. q24h Gastric protectant, prokinetic
■  2–5 mg/kg p.o. q12h
Risedronate sodium ■  0.25 mg/kg p.o. q24h Treatment of satin syndrome
Salbutamol ■  0.05 mg/kg s.c., i.m., i.v. q4–6h Bronchodilator
■  0.1–0.3 mg/ml nebulization (dilute in 10 ml 
saline) q4–6h
Silymarin (milk thistle) ■  4–15 mg/kg p.o. q8–12h Liver disorders
Simeticone ■  0.2 ml/kg p.o. q4–6h (commercial preparation  Anti-gas for stomach and upper gastrointestinal 
for humans) tract

A2.1

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Drug Dose and route Comments
Spironolactone ■  1–2 mg/kg p.o. q12h Diuretic
Sucralfate ■  25–100 mg/kg p.o. q8–12h for 7–10 days Used when gastric ulceration may be present
Can block absorption of other medications and 
nutrients in upper gastrointestinal tract. 
Administer 30 minutes prior to, or 1 hour after, 
other medications and do not combine with 
other oral medications
Terbutaline ■  0.05–0.1 mg/kg i.v., p.o. q8–12h Bronchodilator
Tetracosactide (ACTH) ■  20 IU i.m. as required Diagnosis of hypoadrenocorticism
Thiamazole (methimazole) ■  0.1–5 mg/kg p.o. q12–24h Thyroid neoplasia or hypertrophy
Trilostane ■  2–4 mg/kg p.o. q12h Resolution of clinical signs of 
hyperadrenocorticism
Management of adrenal and pituitary neoplasia
Trimebutine ■  1.5 mg/kg s.c. q8h Motility enhancer
Vitamin C ■  15–30 mg/kg p.o. q24h as a preventative, in  Dissolvable ascorbic acid tablets can be used in 
pregnancy drinking water (1 g/l drinking water q24h). This 
■  50–100 mg/kg i.m., s.c. q24h to treat  route is not recommended: vitamin C rapidly 
deficiency degrades due to exposure to sunlight and 
■  50–200 mg/kg p.o. q24h as a supplement for  contact with metal spouts
ill guinea pigs and for maintenance
Vitamin E ■  5–10 mg/kg p.o. q24h until complete 
resolution
Vitamin K1 ■  1–5 mg/kg s.c. q12–24h as a supplement for  See Appendix 3 for further information about 
liver disease poisoning
■  1–10 mg/kg i.m., p.o. as required for 3 weeks 
for anticoagulant rodenticide poisoning

A2.1

subcutaneous.
